projectName: VEGA v3.4: Knowledge Agent — Phase 1 Lingelpedia Build
description:
Build the Knowledge Agent — VEGA's Truth Engine, Knowledge System, and Sole Write Owner of Lingelpedia. This PRD implements Phase 1 of the VEGA build plan: upgrading Lingelpedia from an Obsidian vault to a Neo4j-powered knowledge graph with dual-write synchronization, claim decomposition, contradiction detection, Agentic Query Mode, and proactive context generation.

BUILDS ON TOP OF v3.3 (ralph/vega-v3.3-telemetry-and-learning branch). The v3.3 branch contains 40 files in src/telemetry/ with 157 passing tests. This PRD adds src/knowledge/ alongside the existing telemetry code.

CRITICAL v3.3 DEPENDENCIES — Import these modules directly:
- TelemetryEmitter (src/telemetry/emitter.ts) — emit telemetry events for all Knowledge Agent operations
- iCloudSync (src/telemetry/icloud-sync.ts) — materialize .icloud stubs before vault scanning
- FrontmatterValidator (src/telemetry/frontmatter-validator.ts) — extend with 7 migration template schemas
- PrivacyAuditor (src/telemetry/privacy-audit.ts) — extend with real Neo4j Cypher queries
- Database (src/telemetry/database.ts) — PostgreSQL connection pool for operational tables
- Tier2Repository (src/telemetry/tier2-repository.ts) — read Tier 2 metrics for model bootstrapping
- All type definitions from src/telemetry/types.ts, tier2-types.ts, etc.

CONVENTIONS (established in v3.3 — follow exactly):
- TypeScript ESM with moduleResolution: bundler
- Zod for runtime validation of all external data
- Non-blocking: try/catch, return null, log to stderr — never crash on external failures
- PostgreSQL BIGINT returns strings — always Number() coercion
- pg ESM import: import pg from 'pg'; const { Pool } = pg;
- Tier 2 upserts: ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE
- vitest with fileParallelism: false for shared DB tests
- ~/vega-telemetry/ base path for telemetry outputs

NEW DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:
- src/knowledge/ — all new Knowledge Agent modules
- src/knowledge/types.ts — Knowledge Agent type definitions
- src/knowledge/neo4j.ts — Neo4j connection and query execution
- src/knowledge/schema.ts — Neo4j schema application (constraints, indexes)
- src/knowledge/embedding.ts — OpenAI embedding pipeline
- src/knowledge/identity.ts — Knowledge Agent identity file parser
- src/knowledge/model-router.ts — frontier/local/embeddings routing
- src/knowledge/migration/ — structured note migration (YAML parsers, entity mappers)
- src/knowledge/decomposition/ — unstructured claim decomposition
- src/knowledge/aqm/ — Agentic Query Mode pipeline (4 stages)
- src/knowledge/proactive/ — Proactive Context Pipeline
- src/knowledge/sync/ — Dual-write synchronization

RUNTIME: IronClaw (M1 Max, 64GB RAM). Repo at ~/Desktop/vega-3.0.
Neo4j: 5.26-community (Docker). Bolt: bolt://localhost:7687. Browser: http://localhost:7474.
Frontier LLM: grok-4-1-fast-reasoning (xAI). Local: qwen3:32b (Ollama). Embeddings: text-embedding-3-small (OpenAI, 1536-dim).
Obsidian vault: ~/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/Lingelpedia
Environment variables: NEO4J_URI, NEO4J_USER, NEO4J_PASSWORD, XAI_API_KEY, LLM_BASE_URL, OPENAI_API_KEY, EMBEDDING_BASE_URL, EMBEDDING_MODEL, VEGA_PG_URL, VEGA_TEST_PG_URL

See VEGA_Implementation_Guide_v3.2.md for full technical specs (Phase 1 build plan: lines 2129-2528, Schema Design: lines 229-420, AQM Pipeline: lines 420-619, Identity Files: lines 660-860, Privacy Audit: lines 900-975, Proactive Context: lines 840-897).

userStories.title: Neo4j Docker setup and schema application
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Neo4j 5.26-community running in Docker with the complete Lingelpedia schema applied so that the knowledge graph is ready for data ingestion. This is the foundation — nothing else works without the graph.
acceptanceCriteria: Neo4j connection module (src/knowledge/neo4j.ts) exists with configurable bolt URI, auth, and connection pooling
Connection uses environment variables: NEO4J_URI (default: bolt://localhost:7687), NEO4J_USER (default: neo4j), NEO4J_PASSWORD (required)
Schema application module (src/knowledge/schema.ts) creates all 5 node type constraints: Claim.id, Entity.id, Source.id, OpenQuestion.id, Bet.id (all UUID uniqueness constraints)
Schema creates vector index: claim_embeddings FOR (c:Claim) ON c.embedding OPTIONS {indexConfig: {`vector.dimensions`: 1536, `vector.similarity_function`: 'cosine'}}
Schema creates full-text index: claim_content FOR (c:Claim) ON EACH [c.content]
Schema creates 8 lookup indexes: claim_domain, claim_status, claim_truth_tier, entity_type, bet_type, bet_status, open_question_status, source_account — per Implementation Guide spec
Schema application is idempotent — running twice produces no errors and no duplicate indexes
Connection health check function returns { connected: boolean, version: string, nodeCount: number }
All Neo4j operations emit telemetry via TelemetryEmitter (import from src/telemetry/emitter.ts) with event_type: 'knowledge_write' or 'knowledge_query'
Test: apply schema → verify all constraints exist via SHOW CONSTRAINTS
Test: apply schema twice → verify no errors (idempotent)
Test: health check returns valid response
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Neo4j is the graph database backbone. The schema must match the Implementation Guide exactly (lines 229-365). Use the official neo4j JavaScript driver (neo4j-driver npm package). The vector index config is critical — 1536 dimensions for OpenAI text-embedding-3-small with cosine similarity.

userStories.title: OpenAI embedding pipeline with Neo4j vector storage
userStories.description: As a developer, I want an embedding pipeline that takes text content, generates 1536-dimensional vectors via OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small, and stores them in Neo4j's native vector index so that semantic search and contradiction detection work across the knowledge graph. Depends on: US-500.
acceptanceCriteria: EmbeddingPipeline module (src/knowledge/embedding.ts) exists that accepts text and returns Float32Array of 1536 dimensions

Uses OpenAI API with model 'text-embedding-3-small' via environment variables: OPENAI_API_KEY, EMBEDDING_BASE_URL (default: https://api.openai.com/v1), EMBEDDING_MODEL (default: text-embedding-3-small)
Batch embedding support: accepts array of texts, returns array of vectors (OpenAI supports up to 2048 inputs per request)
Vector storage: writes embedding to Claim node's embedding property in Neo4j using parameterized Cypher
Semantic search function: given a query text, generates embedding and runs vector similarity search against claim_embeddings index, returns top-K results with similarity scores
Re-embedding function: given a Claim ID, regenerates embedding from current content and updates Neo4j — this is a designed operation per Implementation Guide
Rate limiting: respects OpenAI rate limits with exponential backoff (max 3 retries)
Cost tracking: each embedding call emits telemetry with tokens_used for cost accounting
Non-blocking: embedding failures never block claim creation — claim is created without embedding and flagged for retry
Test: embed a test string → verify 1536-dimensional vector returned
Test: store embedding in Neo4j → run vector similarity search → verify result returned with score
Test: simulate API failure → verify graceful degradation (claim created without embedding)
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: The embedding pipeline connects the text world to the vector world. Every claim gets an embedding for semantic search. The non-blocking requirement is critical — we never fail to store knowledge just because OpenAI is temporarily unreachable. Flag it for retry instead.

userStories.title: Knowledge Agent identity file and model router
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the Knowledge Agent identity file parsed and a model router that directs requests to the correct LLM (frontier, local, or embeddings) based on task type so that expensive frontier model calls are reserved for complex reasoning while routine tasks use the local model. Depends on: US-500.
acceptanceCriteria: Identity file parser (src/knowledge/identity.ts) reads YAML identity file and exposes typed configuration: name, role, personality, ownership, data_access, privacy_duties, model_intelligence, thinking_model (4 perspectives: Analytical, Ambitious, Contrarian, Investigator), north_star, trust_level, operating_algorithm, charter_principles, deletion_policy, tools
Model router (src/knowledge/model-router.ts) routes requests to correct provider based on task classification
Frontier model (grok-4-1-fast-reasoning via xAI): used for AQM, claim decomposition, contradiction detection, initial seeding, complex reasoning. Environment: XAI_API_KEY, LLM_BASE_URL
Local model (qwen3:32b via Ollama): used for routine claim updates, embedding preprocessing, low-stakes formatting, pre-reflections. Environment: OLLAMA_BASE_URL (default: http://localhost:11434)
Embeddings (text-embedding-3-small via OpenAI): routed through EmbeddingPipeline from US-501
Router configuration matches Implementation Guide model_router.yaml: default_model: frontier, cost_optimization: true, supervision_sample_rate: 0.1, quality_threshold: 0.85
Router tracks model usage in telemetry (model_used field in every event)
Fallback behavior: if local model unavailable, route to frontier with cost_warning flag
Test: route a 'claim_decomposition' task → verify frontier model selected
Test: route a 'formatting' task → verify local model selected
Test: simulate local model offline → verify fallback to frontier with warning
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: The model router is the cost control mechanism. Without it, every operation would hit the expensive frontier model. The identity file defines who the Knowledge Agent IS — its perspectives, boundaries, and responsibilities. This is the soul of the agent.

userStories.title: Obsidian vault connection with iCloud sync integration
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the Knowledge Agent to connect to the Obsidian vault at ~/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/Lingelpedia, materializing any .icloud stubs before processing, so that the agent can read all vault content reliably. Depends on: US-500. Uses: iCloudSync from v3.3 (src/telemetry/icloud-sync.ts).
acceptanceCriteria: VaultConnector module (src/knowledge/vault-connector.ts) reads files from the Obsidian vault path
Integrates v3.3 iCloudSync module — calls materialize_icloud_stubs() before any vault scan operation
Vault scan function: recursively lists all .md files, returns array of { path, frontmatter, body, lastModified }
Selective scan: accepts glob patterns to scan specific folders (e.g., 'Finance/Entities/*.md', 'GIX/Meeting Notes/*.md')
File watcher mode: monitors vault for changes using fs.watch, emits events for new/modified/deleted files
Write access restricted to _agent_insights/ directory only — any write attempt outside this directory throws an error
Writes to _agent_insights/ follow Obsidian markdown format with valid YAML frontmatter
YAML frontmatter parsing: extracts frontmatter block (between --- delimiters) and parses to typed object
Body extraction: returns everything after the frontmatter block as raw markdown
Telemetry: all vault operations emit events via TelemetryEmitter
Test: scan a test vault directory → verify file listing with frontmatter and body
Test: attempt write outside _agent_insights/ → verify error thrown
Test: write to _agent_insights/ → verify valid Obsidian markdown produced
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: The vault is the human interface — Mike reads and writes Obsidian notes. The agent reads from the vault and writes ONLY to _agent_insights/. The iCloudSync integration from v3.3 handles the macOS lazy-download problem. Without this, notes Mike wrote on his iPhone might appear as .icloud stubs on IronClaw.

userStories.title: YAML frontmatter parser for all 7 migration template types
userStories.description: As a developer, I want YAML frontmatter parsers for all 7 Obsidian note template types (Entity, Account, Investment, Cash Flow, Institution, Person, and base Claim) so that structured notes can be reliably parsed and mapped to Neo4j nodes. Depends on: US-503. Extends: FrontmatterValidator from v3.3 (src/telemetry/frontmatter-validator.ts).
acceptanceCriteria: MigrationParser module (src/knowledge/migration/parser.ts) extends v3.3 FrontmatterValidator with 7 template-specific Zod schemas
Entity template schema: requires type (entity), name, entity_type (person|organization|financial_instrument|property|concept), domain, optional aliases[], optional description
Account template schema: requires type (account), name, institution, account_type, domain, optional balance, optional currency, optional last_verified
Investment template schema: requires type (investment), name, vehicle, strategy, domain, optional current_value, optional cost_basis, optional inception_date
Cash Flow template schema: requires type (cash_flow), name, direction (inflow|outflow), frequency, amount, source_entity, domain
Institution template schema: requires type (institution), name, institution_type, domain, optional contacts[], optional relationship_status
Person template schema: requires type (person), name, relationship, domain, optional birthday, optional contact_info, optional notes
Base Claim template schema: requires truth_tier (family_direct|multi_source_verified|single_source|agent_inferred), truth_score (0.0-1.0), source_ids[], domain, created_date, last_verified
Parser auto-detects template type from frontmatter 'type' field and applies correct schema
Validation errors return specific field-level messages (e.g., 'truth_tier must be one of: family_direct, multi_source_verified, single_source, agent_inferred')
Test: parse valid Entity frontmatter → verify typed object returned
Test: parse Investment with missing required field → verify specific error message
Test: parse each of the 7 template types → verify all pass with valid data
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: These parsers are the bridge between Obsidian's YAML frontmatter and Neo4j's typed nodes. The schemas must match the Implementation Guide's YAML specs (lines 2280-2420). Extending v3.3's FrontmatterValidator means we inherit the existing validation infrastructure and just add migration-specific schemas.

userStories.title: Structured note migration — Entity and Person mappers
userStories.description: As a developer, I want mappers that convert parsed Entity and Person Obsidian notes into Neo4j Entity nodes with proper relationships so that the structured knowledge from the vault populates the graph. Depends on: US-500, US-501, US-504.
acceptanceCriteria: EntityMapper module (src/knowledge/migration/entity-mapper.ts) converts parsed Entity frontmatter + body into Neo4j MERGE operations
PersonMapper module (src/knowledge/migration/person-mapper.ts) converts parsed Person frontmatter + body into Neo4j Entity nodes with entity_type: 'person'
Both mappers use MERGE (not CREATE) to handle re-runs idempotently — matching on name + entity_type
Entity mapper creates Entity node with all frontmatter fields mapped to Neo4j properties
Person mapper creates Entity node with birthday, relationship, contact_info as properties
Both mappers extract claims from the note body: each paragraph or bullet point becomes a Claim node linked via ABOUT relationship
Claims extracted from structured notes get truth_tier from frontmatter (or 'single_source' default)
Each claim gets an embedding via EmbeddingPipeline (US-501)
Source node created for the Obsidian note itself (source_type: 'obsidian_note', source_account: 'obsidian_vault')
All created claims linked to Source via SOURCED_FROM relationship
Migration statistics returned: { entities_created, entities_updated, claims_created, embeddings_generated, errors }
Test: migrate a test Entity note → verify Entity node and Claim nodes in Neo4j with correct relationships
Test: migrate same note twice → verify no duplicate nodes (MERGE idempotency)
Test: migrate a Person note with birthday → verify Entity node with person-specific properties
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Entity and Person are the most common note types in the existing vault. The MERGE pattern is critical — we're building infrastructure for repeated migration runs. The claim extraction from body text is a simplified version of the full decomposition engine (US-509) — here we just split on paragraphs/bullets rather than doing LLM decomposition.

userStories.title: Structured note migration — Account, Investment, CashFlow, Institution mappers
userStories.description: As a developer, I want mappers for the remaining 4 template types (Account, Investment, CashFlow, Institution) so that financial and institutional knowledge from the vault is fully represented in the graph. Depends on: US-500, US-501, US-504, US-505 (follows same patterns).
acceptanceCriteria: AccountMapper (src/knowledge/migration/account-mapper.ts) converts Account notes to Entity nodes with entity_type: 'account' and links to Institution via BELONGS_TO
InvestmentMapper (src/knowledge/migration/investment-mapper.ts) converts Investment notes to Entity nodes with entity_type: 'financial_instrument' and creates value-tracking Claims
CashFlowMapper (src/knowledge/migration/cashflow-mapper.ts) converts CashFlow notes to Claim nodes with direction, frequency, amount as properties, linked to source Entity via ABOUT
InstitutionMapper (src/knowledge/migration/institution-mapper.ts) converts Institution notes to Entity nodes with entity_type: 'organization' and creates contact-tracking Claims
All mappers follow the same patterns as EntityMapper/PersonMapper: MERGE idempotency, claim extraction from body, embeddings, Source node creation, telemetry emission
AccountMapper creates BELONGS_TO relationship: Account Entity → Institution Entity (auto-creates Institution Entity if not found)
InvestmentMapper creates RELATED_TO relationship to investment vehicle Entity (e.g., WE Fund III)
Cross-domain relationships: if an Account's domain is 'gix' but the Institution is also referenced in 'personal_finance', a RELATED_TO relationship spans domains
Batch migration function: accepts a vault directory path, scans for all template types, runs appropriate mapper for each, returns aggregate statistics
Test: migrate test Account note linked to Institution → verify BELONGS_TO relationship
Test: migrate test Investment note → verify financial_instrument Entity with value Claims
Test: run batch migration on test directory with mixed note types → verify all types processed
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: These mappers complete the structured migration capability. The cross-domain relationship detection is important — Mike's financial life spans personal, GIX, and WE domains, and the graph should capture those connections. The batch migration function is the main entry point for the initial vault import.

userStories.title: Claim decomposition engine for unstructured notes
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a claim decomposition engine that takes unstructured text (meeting notes, journal entries, voice memo transcripts) and decomposes it into atomic, verifiable claims linked to entities so that knowledge from conversational or narrative text is captured in the graph. Depends on: US-500, US-501, US-502.
acceptanceCriteria: ClaimDecomposer module (src/knowledge/decomposition/decomposer.ts) accepts raw text and returns array of structured claims
Uses frontier model (via ModelRouter from US-502) for decomposition — this is complex reasoning, never delegated to local
Decomposition prompt follows Implementation Guide spec: extract atomic claims, identify entities mentioned, assign truth_tier based on source context, estimate truth_score
Each decomposed claim includes: content (natural language), entities (names + types), truth_tier, truth_score_estimate, domain_classification
Entity extraction: identifies persons, organizations, financial instruments, properties, concepts mentioned in each claim
Domain classification: assigns each claim to one of: gix, we, personal_finance, health, family, legal, general

Truth tier assignment rules: text attributed to Mike or Lindsay → family_direct (truth_score ≥ 0.95); text from verified documents → multi_source_verified; single mention → single_source; agent inference → agent_inferred
Deduplication: before creating a Claim node, checks for semantic similarity against existing claims (embedding cosine similarity > 0.95 = potential duplicate, flag for review)
Source tracking: creates Source node for the original text, links all decomposed Claims via SOURCED_FROM
Test: decompose 'Talked to Jim — he mentioned he knows someone at Blackstone' → verify 2+ claims extracted, Jim LaMarche and Blackstone entities identified
Test: decompose a multi-paragraph meeting note → verify atomic claims with correct domain and truth_tier
Test: decompose text containing family member statement → verify family_direct truth_tier assigned
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: This is the core intelligence of the Knowledge Agent. The Jim LaMarche example from the Implementation Guide (lines 366-394) is the canonical test case. The deduplication check via embedding similarity prevents the graph from accumulating redundant knowledge. The frontier model requirement is non-negotiable — claim decomposition is the highest-stakes reasoning the agent does.

userStories.title: Contradiction detection engine
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a contradiction detection engine that identifies conflicting claims in the knowledge graph and surfaces them as OpenQuestion nodes so that the system actively resolves inconsistencies rather than silently harboring them. Depends on: US-500, US-501, US-507.
acceptanceCriteria: ContradictionDetector module (src/knowledge/decomposition/contradiction.ts) scans for conflicts between claims
Uses frontier model for contradiction analysis — complex multi-source reasoning
Two detection modes: on-ingestion (check new claims against existing graph) and periodic-scan (batch check for emergent contradictions)
On-ingestion: when a new claim is created, run vector similarity search for related claims (cosine > 0.7), then use frontier model to assess if any pair is contradictory
Periodic-scan: weekly job that checks high-value claims (truth_tier: family_direct or multi_source_verified) for internal consistency
When contradiction detected: create CONTRADICTS relationship between the two Claims, create OpenQuestion node with the conflict description, link via INVOLVES relationships
OpenQuestion created with: question (what's the conflict), domain, priority (high if family_direct involved, medium otherwise), raised_by: 'knowledge_agent', status: 'open'
Truth tier hierarchy enforcement: family_direct claims are NEVER automatically overwritten by lower-tier claims — contradictions are surfaced for Mike's review
Contradiction severity scoring: same-source contradiction (high), cross-source contradiction (medium), time-decay contradiction (low — old claim vs new claim)
Test: create two contradicting claims about the same entity → verify CONTRADICTS relationship and OpenQuestion created
Test: create claim contradicting a family_direct claim → verify escalation priority is 'high'
Test: create claims that are related but not contradictory → verify no false positive
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Contradiction detection is what makes Lingelpedia a truth engine rather than a knowledge dump. The family_direct protection is paramount — if Mike says something, it's truth. If an agent analysis contradicts Mike, that's an OpenQuestion for Mike to resolve, not an automatic override. The periodic scan catches contradictions that emerge as new knowledge arrives (compound interest at work).

userStories.title: Cross-domain connection discovery (compound interest engine)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the Knowledge Agent to actively discover non-obvious connections between claims and entities across different domains so that the knowledge graph generates compound value — insights that no individual domain could produce alone. Depends on: US-500, US-501, US-507.
acceptanceCriteria: ConnectionDiscovery module (src/knowledge/decomposition/connections.ts) identifies cross-domain relationships
Uses frontier model for connection analysis
On-ingestion mode: when a new claim is created, check if any extracted entities also appear in claims from OTHER domains (cross-domain bridge detection)

Entity co-occurrence analysis: for each entity in a new claim, query Neo4j for all other claims ABOUT that entity in different domains → if found, create RELATED_TO relationship between the cross-domain claims

Implicit bet detection: runs periodic analysis checking for concentration patterns (e.g., all financial positions in USD = implicit currency bet). Uses Cypher queries per Implementation Guide example (lines 396-418)
Implicit bets surfaced as Bet nodes with bet_type: 'implicit', linked to supporting claims via EVIDENCED_BY
Connection scoring: each discovered connection gets a relevance score based on: entity overlap count, domain diversity, recency of claims, truth_tier of claims
High-relevance connections (score > 0.8) emitted as new Claims with truth_tier: 'agent_inferred'
Low-relevance connections (score 0.5-0.8) logged but not persisted — available for periodic review
Test: create claims about Jim LaMarche in personal domain and Blackstone in gix domain → verify cross-domain connection discovered
Test: create multiple USD-denominated financial claims → verify implicit currency bet detection
Test: verify low-relevance connections are NOT persisted as Claims
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: This is the compound interest engine — the reason the Knowledge Agent exists as a centralized truth system rather than siloed per-domain stores. The Jim LaMarche → Blackstone example and the USD concentration bet detection are the canonical test cases from the Implementation Guide. These connections are the 'three weeks later' value that makes the system worth building.

userStories.title: AQM Pipeline — Schema Inspection and Structured Query Construction (Stages 1-2)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the first two stages of the Agentic Query Mode pipeline — Schema Inspection and Structured Query Construction — so that complex questions about the knowledge graph can be translated into precise Cypher queries. Depends on: US-500, US-502.
acceptanceCriteria: AQM module structure: src/knowledge/aqm/pipeline.ts (orchestrator), src/knowledge/aqm/schema-inspector.ts (Stage 1), src/knowledge/aqm/query-constructor.ts (Stage 2)
Query classifier determines if incoming question needs AQM (complex, multi-entity) or simple retrieval (single entity lookup, keyword search)
Stage 1 — Schema Inspection: runs Cypher queries to discover relevant node labels, relationship types, property keys, and data density for the question's domain
Stage 1 Cypher queries include: CALL db.labels(), CALL db.relationshipTypes(), node count per label, property keys per relevant label, sample data for context
Stage 2 — Structured Query Construction: uses frontier model to generate parametric Cypher query based on schema inspection results and the original question
Stage 2 produces parameterized Cypher (no string interpolation — all values as parameters) to prevent injection
Stage 2 supports query patterns from Implementation Guide: multi-entity exposure, cascading impact analysis, temporal change detection
Query validation: generated Cypher is syntax-checked before execution (EXPLAIN query plan without running)
If query construction fails, falls back to vector similarity search as degraded mode
All AQM operations use frontier model exclusively — never delegated to local (per Implementation Guide)
Test: ask 'What is my total exposure to interest rate risk?' → verify Stage 1 inspects financial entities, Stage 2 generates multi-hop Cypher
Test: ask simple 'Who is Jim LaMarche?' → verify query classifier routes to simple retrieval, NOT AQM
Test: verify generated Cypher uses parameters (no string interpolation)
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: AQM Stages 1-2 are the 'understanding and planning' phases. The schema inspection ensures the query is grounded in what actually exists in the graph (not hallucinated relationships). The query constructor turns natural language into precise graph traversals. This is where the graph structure pays off — questions that would require full-text search guesswork in a flat store become deterministic traversals.

userStories.title: AQM Pipeline — Precision Reranking and Grounded Synthesis (Stages 3-4)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the final two stages of the Agentic Query Mode pipeline — Precision Reranking and Grounded Synthesis — so that query results are ranked by relevance and trust, then synthesized into a grounded, cited answer. Depends on: US-510.
acceptanceCriteria: Stage 3 module: src/knowledge/aqm/reranker.ts — scores and reranks query results
Stage 4 module: src/knowledge/aqm/synthesizer.ts — produces grounded answer with citations
Stage 3 scoring formula: score = (semantic_similarity × 0.4) + (truth_tier_weight × 0.35) + (recency_decay × 0.25)
Truth tier weights: family_direct=1.0, multi_source_verified=0.85, single_source=0.6, agent_inferred=0.4
Recency decay: exponential decay from 1.0 (today) toward 0.0 over configurable window (default: 365 days)
Completeness check: Stage 3 identifies gaps — domains or entity types that SHOULD have results but returned empty
Stage 4 — Grounded Synthesis: produces answer with inline citations in format: [Claim ID: {id} | truth_tier: {tier} | truth_score: {score}]

Stage 4 includes: inference chains (how the answer was derived from multiple claims), identified gaps (what we don't know), confidence assessment (overall answer confidence based on component claim scores)
Stage 4 uses frontier model for synthesis — never local
Full pipeline orchestrator: accepts question string, runs all 4 stages in sequence, returns { answer, citations, gaps, confidence, query_used, timing }
Test: run full AQM pipeline against a graph with known claims → verify answer cites correct claims with proper format
Test: verify truth_tier weighting — family_direct claims rank above single_source claims for same semantic similarity
Test: run AQM against empty graph → verify graceful 'no knowledge available' response
Test: run 3 of 5 Implementation Guide test queries (lines 2443-2470) → verify reasonable answers
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: AQM Stages 3-4 are where raw query results become trustworthy answers. The scoring formula encodes our epistemology: what Mike and Lindsay say matters most, verified multi-source claims come next, and agent inferences are treated with appropriate skepticism. The citation format ensures every claim in the answer is traceable to its source. The gap identification is crucial — knowing what we DON'T know is as valuable as knowing what we do.

userStories.title: Proactive Context Pipeline (MOCs, domain summaries, pre-context packages)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the Knowledge Agent to proactively generate context artifacts — Maps of Content, domain summaries, and pre-context packages — so that relevant knowledge is pre-assembled before it's needed rather than only retrieved on demand. Depends on: US-500, US-501, US-502, US-503.
acceptanceCriteria: ProactiveContext module (src/knowledge/proactive/context.ts) generates three artifact types
MOC (Map of Content) generator: when claim density in a topic exceeds configurable threshold (default: 10 claims), auto-generates an index note organizing related claims by theme
MOC format per Implementation Guide: type, theme, generated_at, claims_referenced count, domains_spanned[], key_entities[], open_questions count, active_bets count, staleness assessment
MOC written to Obsidian vault at _agent_insights/MOC_{theme_slug}.md with wiki-linked references to all underlying claims
Domain Summary generator: periodic distillation of changes in a domain since last summary — new_claims, updated_claims, new_contradictions, resolved_questions, key_changes[]
Domain summaries generated weekly for all active domains (domains with ≥1 new claim in the period), daily for high-velocity domains (≥5 claims/day)
Pre-Context Package generator: assembles anticipatory context for upcoming events by scanning calendar data
Pre-context packages assembled 24-48 hours before relevant calendar events, containing: latest claims about attendees/topics, relevant bets, open questions in the event's domain
All generated artifacts written to _agent_insights/ with proper YAML frontmatter
Generation triggers: claim density threshold, calendar events, domain change velocity, scheduled cadence
Test: add 12 claims about 'GIX Series B' → verify MOC auto-generated
Test: add 5 claims in 'personal_finance' domain in one day → verify daily domain summary triggered
Test: create a calendar event with 'CIP' in the title → verify pre-context package assembled with CIP-related claims
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: The proactive context pipeline is what makes the Knowledge Agent feel like an executive assistant rather than a search engine. The pre-context packages for upcoming meetings are the highest-value artifact — imagine walking into a meeting with CIP and having all relevant claims, bets, and open questions pre-assembled. Calendar integration for pre-context requires Google Calendar MCP (configured in US-514).

userStories.title: Dual-write synchronization (Obsidian ↔ Neo4j)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want bidirectional synchronization between Obsidian notes and Neo4j nodes so that changes in either system are reflected in the other within acceptable latency. Depends on: US-500, US-503, US-504, US-505, US-506.
acceptanceCriteria: DualWriteSync module (src/knowledge/sync/dual-write.ts) maintains consistency between Obsidian and Neo4j
Obsidian → Neo4j: when a vault file is modified (detected by VaultConnector watcher from US-503), re-parse frontmatter and body, update corresponding Neo4j nodes
Neo4j → Obsidian: when the Knowledge Agent creates new Claims/Entities/OpenQuestions in Neo4j, generate corresponding Obsidian note in _agent_insights/ with proper frontmatter

Conflict resolution: Obsidian is the human-authored source of truth for manually written notes; Neo4j is the source of truth for agent-generated knowledge. Conflicts flagged as OpenQuestion rather than auto-resolved
Sync latency target: <5 seconds from change detection to both-systems-updated for individual note changes
Batch sync: for bulk operations (initial migration, re-pipeline), sync runs in batch mode with progress tracking and summary statistics
Sync state tracking: maintains a sync ledger in PostgreSQL recording last-synced timestamp per file/node pair for reliable change detection
Sync ledger schema: CREATE TABLE knowledge_sync_ledger (id UUID PRIMARY KEY, obsidian_path TEXT, neo4j_node_id TEXT, last_synced_at TIMESTAMP, sync_direction VARCHAR, sync_status VARCHAR)
Dead letter queue: sync failures are logged to a retry queue rather than silently dropped. Retries with exponential backoff (max 3 attempts).
Test: modify an Obsidian note → verify Neo4j node updated within 5 seconds
Test: create a new Claim in Neo4j → verify _agent_insights/ note created in Obsidian
Test: simulate sync conflict (Obsidian and Neo4j modified simultaneously) → verify OpenQuestion created
Test: simulate sync failure → verify dead letter queue entry and retry
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Dual-write is the architectural invariant that keeps Obsidian (human interface) and Neo4j (machine intelligence) in sync. The 'don't kill Obsidian' principle from the Implementation Guide means Mike can always open Obsidian and see his knowledge. The sync ledger in PostgreSQL is critical for reliability — without it, we'd have to do full scans to detect changes.

userStories.title: Google OAuth connectors (Calendar, Gmail, Drive — read-only)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want MCP server connections to Google Calendar, Gmail, and Google Drive using Mike's personal Google account so that the Knowledge Agent can read calendar events, emails, and documents as Phase 1 data feeds. Depends on: US-500.
acceptanceCriteria: GoogleConnectors module (src/knowledge/connectors/google.ts) wraps MCP server connections for Google services
Google Calendar: read access via MCP, retrieves events within a configurable time window (default: past 30 days + future 14 days)
Calendar events returned as typed objects: { id, title, start, end, attendees[], location, description }
Gmail: read access via MCP, retrieves emails matching configurable search criteria (default: inbox, last 7 days)
Emails returned as typed objects: { id, from, to, subject, body, date, labels[], attachments_count }
Google Drive: read access via MCP, scoped to specific folders (GIX, WE, Finance). Returns document metadata and content
Drive documents returned as: { id, name, mimeType, content, folder, lastModified }
OAuth token management: stores refresh token securely, handles token refresh automatically
All connector operations emit telemetry events
Rate limiting: respects Google API quotas with exponential backoff
Privacy boundary enforcement: connectors are read-only — no write operations to Google services in Phase 1
Test: retrieve calendar events for a date range → verify typed response
Test: retrieve recent emails → verify typed response with all fields
Test: attempt write operation → verify error (read-only enforcement)
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Google connectors are Phase 1 data feeds. The Knowledge Agent reads from these sources to build the knowledge graph — a calendar event about a CIP meeting becomes claims about scheduling, attendees, and context. MS365 connectors are deferred to Phase 3. OAuth setup requires manual browser auth from Mike — ralph should build the code that uses the tokens, and the test can mock the MCP responses.

userStories.title: Privacy audit with real Neo4j Cypher queries
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the privacy audit infrastructure to run actual Cypher queries against the Neo4j knowledge graph, extending the v3.3 PrivacyAuditor with the 6 production audit queries specified in the Implementation Guide. Depends on: US-500. Extends: PrivacyAuditor from v3.3 (src/telemetry/privacy-audit.ts).
acceptanceCriteria: KnowledgePrivacyAuditor module (src/knowledge/privacy-auditor.ts) extends v3.3 PrivacyAuditor
Audit 1 — Sole Write Owner: MATCH (c:Claim) WHERE c.created_by <> 'knowledge_agent' RETURN count(c) — Expected: 0
Audit 2 — Children's Data Protection (Harrison): MATCH (e:Entity {name: 'Harrison Lingle'})<-[:ABOUT]-(c:Claim) WHERE NOT c.protection_level = 'children_elevated' RETURN count(c) — Expected: 0
Audit 3 — Children's Data Protection (Beckham): same pattern for Beckham Lingle — Expected: 0
Audit 4 — Connector Scope (Drive): MATCH (s:Source) WHERE s.source_account = 'google_drive' AND NOT s.folder IN ['GIX', 'WE', 'Finance'] RETURN count(s) — Expected: 0
Audit 5 — Cross-Domain Leakage (Health): MATCH (c:Claim {domain: 'health'})-[:SOURCED_FROM]->(s:Source) WHERE s.source_account NOT IN ['obsidian_vault', 'apple_health'] RETURN count(c) — Expected: 0

Audit 6 — Agent Access Anomaly: MATCH (c:Claim) WHERE c.last_accessed_by IS NOT NULL AND c.last_accessed_by <> 'knowledge_agent' RETURN agent, domain, count — cross-reference against identity file declarations
All queries parameterized (no hardcoded values)
Non-zero results on Audits 1-5 trigger Level 3 escalation (immediate Mike notification via bar_raiser_direct table)
Audit results written to privacy_audit_log PostgreSQL table (schema per Implementation Guide lines 916-929)
Audit schedule: configurable, default weekly. Runs automatically via scheduled job
Test: insert a Claim with created_by <> 'knowledge_agent' → run Audit 1 → verify detection
Test: insert a Claim about Harrison without children_elevated → run Audit 2 → verify detection
Test: run full audit suite against clean graph → verify all pass
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Privacy is non-negotiable for the Lingle family system. The children's data protection audits (Harrison and Beckham) are the highest priority. These queries are the automated enforcement of Charter Principle 7 (Privacy and Protection Duty). The v3.3 PrivacyAuditor built the framework; this story fills it with real Neo4j queries.

userStories.title: Performance-Based Model Bootstrapping (frontier → local supervision loop)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a supervision loop that tracks when the local model (qwen3:32b) can safely handle tasks currently routed to the frontier model (grok-4-1-fast-reasoning) so that operational costs decrease over time as the local model proves its quality. Depends on: US-502.
acceptanceCriteria: ModelBootstrapper module (src/knowledge/model-bootstrapper.ts) implements 5-step supervision loop per Implementation Guide (lines 789-837)
Step 1: Frontier does work first — all tasks start on frontier model
Step 2: Local attempts same task (shadow mode) — local model processes the same input in parallel but output is NOT used
Step 3: Quality comparison — compare frontier and local outputs using configurable quality metrics (semantic similarity, structural match, correctness indicators)
Step 4: If local quality meets threshold (quality_threshold: 0.85), mark task type as 'local_candidate'
Step 5: After N successful local_candidate evaluations (default: 10), delegate task type to local with permanent supervision (supervision_sample_rate: 0.1 = 10% spot-check)

model_quality_metrics PostgreSQL table tracks delegation status per task_type: task_type, model, quality_score, sample_count, delegation_status (frontier_only, shadow_testing, local_candidate, delegated), last_evaluated
Bar Raiser monitoring: if delegated task quality drops below threshold, auto-revert to frontier_only with alert
Cost tracking: tracks cost savings from delegation (frontier cost - local cost per delegated task)
Test: run 5-step loop for a 'formatting' task type → verify progression through delegation statuses
Test: simulate quality drop on delegated task → verify auto-revert to frontier
Test: verify cost tracking correctly calculates savings
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Model bootstrapping is the cost optimization engine. Day 1, everything runs on the expensive frontier model. Over time, routine tasks get delegated to the local model as it proves quality. The 10% permanent supervision ensures quality doesn't silently degrade. This is one of the most Amazonian concepts in the whole system — trust but verify, then keep verifying.

userStories.title: Operational PostgreSQL tables (trust levels, knowledge edit queue, sync ledger)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the operational PostgreSQL tables that the Knowledge Agent needs for trust level tracking, knowledge edit queue management, and sync state so that these subsystems have reliable persistent storage. Depends on: US-513.
acceptanceCriteria: Trust levels table created per Implementation Guide (lines 2650-2661): id UUID, agent_name VARCHAR, integration VARCHAR, trust_level VARCHAR (observe|suggest|assist|act), granted_at TIMESTAMP, granted_by VARCHAR, evidence TEXT, revoked_at TIMESTAMP, revoked_reason TEXT

Knowledge edit queue table created per Implementation Guide (lines 2845-2860): id UUID, proposing_agent VARCHAR, edit_type VARCHAR, target_node_id VARCHAR, target_node_type VARCHAR, current_value TEXT, proposed_value TEXT, reasoning TEXT, confidence FLOAT, created_at TIMESTAMP, reviewed_at TIMESTAMP, reviewed_by VARCHAR, status VARCHAR DEFAULT 'pending', resolution_notes TEXT
Knowledge sync ledger table created per US-513 spec: id UUID, obsidian_path TEXT, neo4j_node_id TEXT, last_synced_at TIMESTAMP, sync_direction VARCHAR, sync_status VARCHAR

Bar Raiser direct table created per Implementation Guide (lines 2743-2754): id UUID, severity VARCHAR, flag_type VARCHAR, target_agent VARCHAR, evidence TEXT, recommended_action TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMP, acknowledged_at TIMESTAMP, resolved_at TIMESTAMP, resolution TEXT

Privacy audit log table created per Implementation Guide (lines 916-929): id UUID, audit_type VARCHAR, status VARCHAR, finding TEXT, affected_data TEXT, affected_agent VARCHAR, recommended_action TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMP, acknowledged_at TIMESTAMP, resolved_at TIMESTAMP, resolution TEXT
Model quality metrics table for bootstrapping: task_type VARCHAR, model VARCHAR, quality_score FLOAT, sample_count INT, delegation_status VARCHAR, last_evaluated TIMESTAMP
All tables created idempotently (IF NOT EXISTS)
Migration script that creates all tables in correct order
Test: run migration → verify all 6 tables exist with correct schemas
Test: run migration twice → verify idempotent (no errors)
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: These tables are the operational backbone. Some already have v3.3 dependencies (e.g., the privacy audit framework), but the actual table creation was deferred because v3.3 was telemetry-focused. Now that we're building the Knowledge Agent, these tables need to exist. The knowledge_edit_queue is future-facing (domain agents will use it in Phase 3+) but the schema should be created now.

userStories.title: End-to-end validation: compound value test and Phase 1 success criteria gate
userStories.description: As a developer, I want integration tests that validate the complete Knowledge Agent pipeline and verify all Phase 1 success criteria are met so that we have confidence the system works as a coherent whole before proceeding to Phase 2. Depends on: US-500 through US-517.
acceptanceCriteria: Test 1 — Structured migration: migrate ≥20 test notes across all 7 template types → verify ≥100 Claim nodes in Neo4j with correct relationships
Test 2 — Unstructured decomposition: decompose 5 meeting notes → verify atomic claims extracted with entities and relationships
Test 3 — Contradiction detection: insert 2 contradicting claims → verify CONTRADICTS relationship and OpenQuestion created
Test 4 — Cross-domain discovery: insert claims about same entity in 2 domains → verify cross-domain RELATED_TO relationship discovered
Test 5 — Implicit bet detection: insert 5 USD-denominated financial claims → verify implicit currency bet detected
Test 6 — AQM full pipeline: ask 3 of 5 Implementation Guide test queries → verify grounded answers with citations
Test 7 — Embedding search: embed 20 claims → run semantic search for a query → verify relevant claims returned ranked by score
Test 8 — Privacy audit: run full audit suite against graph with 1 known violation → verify detection and escalation
Test 9 — Dual-write: modify an Obsidian test note → verify Neo4j updated; create a Neo4j claim → verify _agent_insights/ note created
Test 10 — Proactive context: trigger MOC generation with 12 claims in same theme → verify MOC file created in _agent_insights/

Phase 1 success criteria (all must pass): ≥100 Claim nodes in graph, ≥10 cross-domain relationships discovered, ≥3 contradictions detected and surfaced, AQM answers ≥3/5 test queries correctly, privacy audit shows 0 violations on clean data, dual-write latency <5 seconds
All results logged to _agent_insights/v34_validation_report.md
Typecheck passes across entire src/knowledge/ directory
userStories.notes: This is the Phase 1 gate check. Every subsystem must demonstrate it works in integration, not just isolation. The success criteria come directly from the Implementation Guide Phase 1 spec (lines 2498-2520). If any criterion fails, Phase 2 (VEGA-Core + Bar Raiser) does not proceed. The compound value test (Tests 4-5) is the most important — it proves the knowledge graph generates insights that no single data source could produce alone.
